rows.v: A quick, straight punch thrown with the lead hand. The most fundamental punch — used to measure distance, set up combinations, and keep opponents at bay.
He kept his opponent off balance all night with a stiff, constant jab.
From 'jab' (a sharp, abrupt poke). The lead-hand jab is boxing's most-thrown and most-important punch, the foundation of nearly every offense.
A powerful straight punch thrown with the rear hand, crossing the body. Often paired with the jab in the classic 'one-two' combination.
She snapped a jab, then drove the cross straight down the middle for the knockdown.
Named because the rear hand travels 'across' the body to the target. As the 'two' in the one-two, it's a fighter's bread-and-butter power punch.
A punch thrown in a horizontal arc, bent at the elbow, targeting the side of the head or body. Devastating when it lands clean on the chin.
He ducked the jab and came back with a vicious left hook to the temple.
From the 'hook' shape of the bent arm and curving path. The hook's sideways angle slips around an opponent's guard to land on the jaw or ribs.
A vertical punch thrown upward, traveling under an opponent's guard to land on the chin. Especially effective at close range.
As they clinched, she ripped an uppercut up the middle that snapped his head back.
Descriptive: the punch cuts 'up' from below. Its rising path slips inside a high guard, making it a brutal weapon in close, infighting exchanges.
A series of punches thrown in quick succession as a coordinated sequence. Combos overwhelm defenses far better than single shots.
He unloaded a four-punch combination, the flurry too fast for his opponent to block.
From 'combine' — punches 'combined' into a flowing sequence. Stringing strikes together (the 'combo') is how fighters break through a solid guard.
A punch aimed at the torso rather than the head. Body shots sap an opponent's stamina and will over time — 'kill the body, the head will die.'
Round after round of body shots wore him down until his hands finally dropped.
Descriptive: a shot to the 'body'. A patient body attack is a classic strategy — the damage accumulates, slowly draining an opponent's energy and resolve.
A looping power punch thrown over the top with the rear hand, arcing down onto the target. A favorite of fighters looking for a knockout.
The shorter fighter landed a huge overhand right that dropped his taller opponent.
Descriptive: the punch comes 'over' the top in a downward arc. Its looping path can sail over a jab to land flush, a notorious knockout blow.
A 'counterpunch' is thrown immediately in response to an opponent's attack, exploiting the opening their punch creates. The art of the counter-puncher.
She slipped his jab and countered instantly with a cross to the exposed chin.
From 'counter' (to respond or oppose). Great counter-punchers turn an opponent's offense against them, landing in the gap their attack leaves behind.
The defensive position of the hands and arms protecting the head and body. A 'high guard' shields the head; styles vary from peek-a-boo to the long guard.
He kept a tight guard, hands high, weathering the storm of punches.
From 'guard' (to protect). The hand-and-arm position is a fighter's first line of defense; different guard styles reflect different defensive philosophies.
A defensive move where a fighter shifts their head just outside the line of a punch, making it miss by inches. Pure economy of movement.
She slipped the jab by a hair and was already loading her counter.
From 'slip' (to slide smoothly aside). The subtle head movement evades a punch without blocking it, leaving both hands free to counter instantly.
A defensive technique of bending the knees ('bobbing') and moving the upper body in a rolling motion ('weaving') to duck under and avoid punches.
He bobbed and weaved under the looping hooks, slipping inside to land body shots.
Descriptive of the two motions: 'bobbing' down and 'weaving' side to side. Together they let a fighter duck beneath punches and work into close range.
A defensive move that deflects an incoming punch aside with a small, well-timed movement of the hand, rather than absorbing or fully blocking it.
She parried his jab with a flick of her glove, redirecting it harmlessly past her face.
From Italian 'parare' (to ward off), via fencing. A parry redirects rather than absorbs the blow, conserving energy and opening counter opportunities.
The skill of moving the feet to control distance, angle, and balance. Great footwork lets a fighter attack and escape while staying balanced.
His dazzling footwork let him dart in, land, and slide out before any counter could land.
Descriptive: the 'work' done by the 'feet'. Often called the foundation of everything — a fighter who can't move well can't attack or defend effectively.
A close-quarters hold where fighters grab each other to smother offense, rest, or set up other techniques. In MMA, a key position for strikes and takedowns.
Tired and hurt, he tied his opponent up in a clinch to survive the round.
From 'clinch' (to grip tightly). In boxing it neutralizes offense and buys recovery time; in MMA the clinch becomes a battleground for knees, elbows, and takedowns.
A fake movement designed to provoke a reaction from an opponent, creating an opening for the real attack. Selling a punch you don't throw.
He feinted the jab, drew the block, then landed the real shot underneath.
From French 'feindre' (to pretend). A feint is a lie told with the body — faking one action to manipulate the opponent into exposing a target.
An MMA and wrestling technique to bring a standing opponent down to the mat, shifting the fight from striking to grappling. Controls where the fight happens.
She shot in for a takedown, driving through his hips and slamming him to the canvas.
Descriptive: 'taking' an opponent 'down' to the ground. A successful takedown lets a grappler impose their game and escape a striker's range.
On the ground, the position of the fighter on their back using their legs to control the opponent between them. A defensive position that can also attack.
Flat on his back, he played an active guard, hunting for a submission from underneath.
Borrowed from grappling: the bottom fighter 'guards' against the top with their legs. Far from helpless, a skilled guard threatens sweeps and submissions.
A dominant ground position where a fighter sits atop a prone opponent's torso, freeing their hands to strike or attack with submissions.
Once she took mount, she rained down punches from the top with the fight nearly hers.
From 'mount' (to climb on top of). One of grappling's most dominant positions — the top fighter controls the action and the bottom struggles to escape.
A technique — a choke or joint lock — that forces an opponent to surrender ('tap out') or risk injury or unconsciousness. A way to win without striking.
He locked in a tight armbar, and his opponent had no choice but to submit.
From 'submit' (to yield). A submission ends a fight through technique rather than force, compelling surrender via choke or joint manipulation.
Striking a downed opponent from a top grappling position with punches and elbows. A staple offensive tactic of MMA.
From side control, she opened up with ground and pound until the referee stepped in.
Descriptive: 'pounding' an opponent on the 'ground'. The phrase captures MMA's blend of grappling control and striking, raining blows from a dominant top position.
A defensive reaction to a takedown attempt, where a fighter throws their legs back and drops their hips to avoid being taken down. The striker's best friend.
He read the shot early and sprawled hard, stuffing the takedown and staying on his feet.
From 'sprawl' (to spread out). By spreading the legs back and dropping weight onto the attacker, a fighter 'stuffs' the takedown and keeps the fight standing.
The rear-naked choke — a submission applied from behind, wrapping an arm around the opponent's neck to cut off blood flow. One of the most effective chokes.
She took his back, slid her arm under his chin, and the rear-naked choke ended it.
The 'naked' refers to using no gi/collar (bare arms), applied from the 'rear'. Among the most reliable and feared submissions in all of grappling.
A 'KO' — when a fighter is knocked unconscious or cannot rise before the referee's ten-count. The most decisive way to win a fight.
One clean overhand right ended the night with a brutal first-round knockout.
Descriptive: the opponent is 'knocked out' of the fight. The KO is boxing and MMA's definitive, dramatic finish — no judges, no doubt.
Technical Knockout — when a fight is stopped because a fighter can't safely continue, though they aren't unconscious. The referee, corner, or doctor calls it.
The referee waved off the fight for a TKO as the defenseless fighter stopped firing back.
Distinguished from a clean KO: 'technically' a stoppage rather than an unconscious knockout. It protects fighters who are hurt and no longer defending themselves.
When a fight goes the full distance, the winner is determined by the judges' scorecards. The outcome rests on rounds won rather than a finish.
With no knockout, the bout went to a decision, and the judges' cards decided the winner.
From the judges' 'decision'. When neither fighter finishes the other, scoring each round determines who did enough to earn the nod.
A 'unanimous' decision means all judges agree on the winner; a 'split' decision means they disagree, with the majority deciding. Split decisions signal a close fight.
It was so close it went to a split decision, two judges for her and one against.
Descriptive: the judges are 'unanimous' (all agree) or 'split' (divided). A split decision marks a razor-thin contest that could have gone either way.
A timed segment of a fight (typically 3 minutes), separated by rest. Bouts run a set number of rounds — championship fights often go twelve in boxing, five in MMA.
He saved his energy, knowing he had to pace himself across all twelve rounds.
From the sense of a 'round' or cycle of action. Dividing fights into rounds with rest between shapes pacing, strategy, and the scoring of the bout.
The official weighing of fighters before a bout to confirm they meet their weight-class limit. A tense ritual, sometimes more dramatic than the fight.
He barely made the limit at the weigh-in after a grueling final day of cutting weight.
Descriptive: the fighters are 'weighed in' to the contest. Making weight is its own brutal discipline, and the weigh-in's face-offs build the fight's drama.
To signal surrender by physically tapping the opponent or mat, conceding a submission. The instant, unambiguous way to quit before injury.
Caught in the armbar, she tapped out before her elbow could give way.
Descriptive: the fighter 'taps' to submit. The tap is grappling's universal surrender signal, ending a fight the moment a submission becomes inescapable.
A wild, looping, all-or-nothing power punch thrown with maximum force and little technique. Dangerous if it lands, reckless if it misses.
He swung a desperate haymaker that, by luck, caught his opponent flush and ended it.
From the wide, swinging motion resembling scything hay with a tool. A haymaker trades accuracy for raw power — a knockout if it lands, a gamble always.
Slang for a fighter who is easily knocked out, unable to absorb punches to the chin. The opposite of a fighter with a 'good chin.'
Talented as he was, his glass jaw meant one clean shot could always end his night.
A vivid metaphor: the jaw is as fragile as 'glass', shattering under a blow. A fighter's chin (ability to take a punch) is among their most prized traits.
A fighter pinned against the ring ropes and absorbing punishment, in serious trouble. Figuratively, anyone in a desperate, losing situation.
She had him on the ropes, teeing off as he covered up and prayed for the bell.
From a fighter literally being trapped against the 'ropes' of the ring, unable to escape. The phrase spread into everyday speech for being in dire straits.
A left-handed fighter who leads with the right hand and stands in a mirror-image stance. Their unusual angles trouble orthodox opponents.
The orthodox boxer struggled all night against the southpaw's awkward, reversed angles.
Possibly from baseball, where left-handed pitchers faced south in old ballparks, their throwing arm to the 'south'. Southpaws frustrate fighters used to right-handers.
A way of ranking fighters' skill independent of weight class — imagining them all the same size. The mythical answer to 'who's the best, regardless of weight?'
Though a lightweight, she was considered the best pound-for-pound fighter in the world.
A thought experiment: comparing fighters 'pound for pound' as if equal in size. It honors pure skill, letting a small fighter top a list over giants.
To 'gas out' is to run out of stamina and energy mid-fight, leaving a fighter exhausted and vulnerable. Conditioning's cruelest failure.
He swung for the fences early, gassed out by the third round, and had nothing left.
From running out of 'gas' (fuel). A fighter who empties the tank too soon 'gasses out', their offense and defense crumbling as exhaustion takes over.
